examples.title: Algorithm: Student Class with equals, hashCode, and compareTo
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how to implement equals(), hashCode(), and compareTo() methods for storing user-defined classes in collections
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Student Class Implementation
INPUT: Student object with rollNo and name fields
OUTPUT: Properly comparable and hashable Student objects

BEGIN CLASS Student
 // Private fields
 DECLARE PRIVATE int rollNo
 DECLARE PRIVATE String name
 
 // Constructor
 CONSTRUCTOR(rollNo, name)
 SET this.rollNo = rollNo
 SET this.name = name
 END CONSTRUCTOR
 
 // equals() method - defines object equality
 FUNCTION equals(obj)
 IF obj is null THEN RETURN false
 IF this == obj THEN RETURN true
 IF getClass() != obj.getClass() THEN RETURN false
 
 DECLARE Student other = CAST obj to Student
 RETURN this.rollNo == other.rollNo
 END FUNCTION
 
 // hashCode() method - must be consistent with equals()
 FUNCTION hashCode()
 RETURN hash(rollNo) // Use Objects.hash() for multiple fields
 END FUNCTION
 
 // compareTo() method - defines natural ordering
 FUNCTION compareTo(other)
 RETURN compare(this.rollNo, other.rollNo)
 END FUNCTION
END CLASS

// Using in Collections
ALGORITHM Using Student in Collections
BEGIN
 // ArrayList - works with any objects
 DECLARE ArrayList<Student> list = new ArrayList()
 list.add(student1)
 list.add(student2)
 
 // HashSet - relies on equals() and hashCode()
 DECLARE HashSet<Student> set = new HashSet()
 set.add(student1) // Uses equals() to check duplicates
 
 // TreeSet - relies on compareTo()
 DECLARE TreeSet<Student> treeSet = new TreeSet()
 treeSet.add(student1) // Uses compareTo() for sorting
 
 // HashMap - uses equals() and hashCode() for keys
 DECLARE HashMap<Student, Integer> map = new HashMap()
 map.put(student1, 100)
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode outlines the three critical methods needed when storing user-defined classes in Java collections. The equals() method defines what makes two objects equal (based on rollNo), hashCode() ensures consistent hashing for hash-based collections like HashSet and HashMap, and compareTo() enables natural ordering for TreeSet and TreeMap. The key contract is that if two objects are equal according to equals(), they must have the same hashCode.

examples.title: Java: Student Class with equals(), hashCode(), and compareTo()
examples.description: Complete Java implementation showing how to properly store user-defined classes in ArrayList, HashSet, TreeSet, and HashMap
examples.explanation: This comprehensive Java implementation demonstrates storing user-defined Student objects in various collections. The equals() method defines equality based on rollNo, hashCode() returns the same value for equal objects (required contract), and compareTo() enables natural ordering. HashSet uses equals() and hashCode() to detect duplicates, TreeSet uses compareTo() for sorting and duplicate detection, and HashMap uses these methods for key management. The output shows that s1 and s3 (same rollNo) are treated as duplicates in HashSet and HashMap.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for HashSet/HashMap operations, O(n) for ArrayList contains, O(log n) for TreeSet
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for all collections where n is number of elements

examples.title: Python: User-Defined Classes in Collections
examples.description: Python equivalent showing how to make custom classes work with sets, dictionaries, and sorting
examples.code:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any

# Python equivalent of the Java Student class
@dataclass # Automatically generates __init__, __repr__, __eq__
class Student:
 roll_no: int
 name: str
 gpa: float
 
 # Custom hash function - must be immutable and consistent with __eq__
 def __hash__(self):
 return hash(self.roll_no)

# Python's dataclass automatically generates __eq__ based on fields
# But we can customize it if needed

class StudentCustom:
 """Manual implementation showing the concepts clearly"""
 
 def __init__(self, roll_no: int, name: str, gpa: float):
 self.roll_no = roll_no
 self.name = name
 self.gpa = gpa
 
 # __eq__ - defines equality (like Java's equals())
 def __eq__(self, other: Any) -> bool:
 if not isinstance(other, StudentCustom):
 return False
 return self.roll_no == other.roll_no
 
 # __hash__ - must be consistent with __eq__ (like Java's hashCode())
 def __hash__(self):
 return hash(self.roll_no)
 
 # __lt__ - for sorting (like Java's compareTo())
 def __lt__(self, other: 'StudentCustom') -> bool:
 return self.roll_no < other.roll_no
 
 # __repr__ - for string representation
 def __repr__(self):
 return f"StudentCustom(roll_no={self.roll_no}, name='{self.name}', gpa={self.gpa})"


def demonstrate_collections():
 # Create student objects
 s1 = StudentCustom(101, "Alice", 3.8)
 s2 = StudentCustom(102, "Bob", 3.5)
 s3 = StudentCustom(101, "Charlie", 3.9) # Same roll_no as s1
 s4 = Student(101, "Diana", 4.0)
 
 print("=== List (like ArrayList) ===")
 student_list = [s1, s2, s3]
 print(f"List: {student_list}")
 print(f"s1 in list: {s1 in student_list}") # Uses __eq__
 
 print("\n=== Set (like HashSet) ===")
 student_set = {s1, s2, s3}
 print(f"Set size: {len(student_set)}") # 2, because s1 and s3 are equal
 print(f"Set: {student_set}")
 
 print("\n=== Dictionary (like HashMap) ===")
 student_dict = {}
 student_dict[s1] = "Scholarship"
 student_dict[s2] = "None"
 student_dict[s3] = "Grant" # Overwrites s1's value
 print(f"Dict: {student_dict}")
 print(f"Value for s1: {student_dict.get(s1)}")
 
 print("\n=== Sorted (like TreeSet) ===")
 sorted_students = sorted([s2, s1, s3], key=lambda x: x.roll_no)
 print(f"Sorted by roll_no: {sorted_students}")
 
 # Using dataclass with hash
 print("\n=== Dataclass with hash ===")
 dc_set = {s4} # Works because we defined __hash__
 print(f"Dataclass set: {dc_set}")
 
 print("\n=== Equality checks ===")
 print(f"s1 == s3: {s1 == s3}") # True (same roll_no)
 print(f"hash(s1) == hash(s3): {hash(s1) == hash(s3)}") # True
 
 # Important: if two objects are equal, they MUST have same hash
 s5 = StudentCustom(101, "Eve", 3.0)
 print(f"s1 == s5: {s1 == s5}") # True
 print(f"hash(s1) == hash(s5): {hash(s1) == hash(s5)}") # True (contract maintained)


if __name__ == "__main__":
 demonstrate_collections()
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates the equivalent concepts for storing user-defined classes in collections. The __eq__ method (like Java's equals()) defines equality based on roll_no, __hash__ (like hashCode()) must return the same value for equal objects, and __lt__ (like compareTo()) enables sorting. Python sets and dictionaries require proper __hash__ and __eq__ implementation to work correctly. The dataclass decorator provides automatic implementations, but we can customize them as shown. The key contract is identical to Java: equal objects must have the same hash value.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) average for set/dict operations, O(n) for list operations
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for all collections where n is number of elements
